Practices Cornrnlssron

77o L Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, cA 95814
December 11, 1990
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our Fil-e No. I -9 0-653
Dear Mr. BeIl:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing the order and subsequent stay issued in Service Emplovees
rnternational Union, AFL-Cro' et al. v. Fair PoIitical Practices
Commission, United States oistrict court, Eastern District of
california, case No. cIvS-89-0433, LKK-JFM (hereafter, 
SEIU") and
the decj,sion's effect on the campaign provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the 'ras1"1.1/ Since you have requested inforrnal
interin advice, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.2 /
OUESTIONS
1. what is the effect of the SEIU decision on persons and
cornmittees which nake contributions to legislative candidates?
1/ covernment code sections 81ooo-91015. A1I statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
conrnission regulations aPpear at 2 california code of Regufations
Section 18ooo, et seq. All references to regulations are to Titfe
2, Divisj.on 6 of the california code of Regulations
2/ In a telephone conversation with ur. Ahuja of our office on
october 11, 1990, you had withdrawn your request for an opinion
pending receipt of this advice letter, but reserved the option to
rene$/ it later. 
Informal assistance does not provide the
requestor with the inrnunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322'5660


Our Fi■e No. 
■-90-653
Page 2
2. what is the effect of the SEIU decision on persons and
comnittees which make independent expenditures in support of or in
opposition to legisLative candidates?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Persons and cormittees thich make contributions to
legislative candidates are stilL subject to the provisions of
Proposition 73 as interpreted prior to the final order in the SEIU
case .
2. Persons and conmittees which make independent
expenditures in support of or in opposj.tion to legislative
candidates are stilL subject to the provisions of Proposition 73
as interpreted prior to the final order in the SEIU case.
DI SCUSS TON
In June of 1988, Propositions 68 and 73 were offered to the
voters of California as amendnents to the Act. While both
initiatives 
were adopted by the voters, Proposition 73 received a
higher number of votes and pursuant to the Cal-ifornia constitu-
tion, Article II, Section 10(b), conflicting provisions of
Proposition 58 were suPerseded.
In November of 1988, the comnission issued the BelI opinion.
(In re BeIL (1988) 11 FPPC Ops. 1, copy attached.) In the BeI1
opinion the commission determined that most of Proposition 68 lras
superseded by conflicting provisions of Proposition 73. Ilo\.rever,
at the July conmission rneeting, Connission staff subnitted its
analysis concerning whether provisions of Proposition 68 would
becorne operative in the event the federal district 
court
invalidated provisions of Proposition 73. The mernorandum
concluded that the otherwise suspended provisions of Proposition
68 woul-d be revived and enforceable if the provisions of
Proposition 73, v/j-th $/hich they originally conflicted, were held
unconstitutiona I by the court.
On September 25, !99o, the United States District Court
issued an order in the SE[g case which invalidated portions of the
Act vrhich were added by Proposition 73 in June of 1988, including
the fiscal year contribution linitations of the Act and the
transfer ban of Section 85304.
However, on Septenber 28, the federal court stayed executj,on
of the order with respect to legislative candidates until "there
is a final judgnent that the campaign contribution lirnitations of
Proposition 68 are not effective.rr Since the provisions of
Proposition 68 apply only to Legislative candidates, the stay


Our Fi■e No. I-90-653
Page 3
effectively prevented the revival of any Provisions of Proposi.tion
6a. Consequently, under the stay, Ieqislative candidates and
persons and committees which make contributions to, or independent
expendj,tures on behaLf of legislative candidates are sti11 subject
to the provisions of Proposition 73 as interpreted prior to the
september 25th order in the EELq case.
on November L, 1-990, the california Supreme court issued its
decision in Taxpavers to Limit Campaiqn Spendinq v. Fair Po]itical
Practices conmission, case No. 5012016. on November 20, 1990, in
response to a petition for rehearing filed by plaintiffs,
Taxpayers to Linit Campaign Spending, the Supreme Court issued an
order extending the tirne for granting or denying the petition to
no later than JaDuary 30, 1991. - ,consequently, the federal court's
stay continues to be effective.r/
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact ne at (91-5) 322-5901 .
